scenes.title: Welcome to the Animal Kingdom
scenes.narration: Welcome, young explorer! Today we're going on an exciting adventure to learn how animals live, eat, breathe, and move in different environments. From the freezing Arctic to the deepest oceans, animals have amazing features that help them survive!

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Did you know that a polar bear's fur is actually transparent, not white? It just looks white because of how light reflects off it! Animals are full of surprises. Let's discover their secrets together!

scenes.fun_fact: There are over 8.7 million species of animals on Earth, and scientists discover new ones every year!

scenes.title: What is a Habitat?
scenes.narration: A habitat is the natural home of an animal where it gets food, water, shelter, and everything it needs to survive. Different animals live in different habitats based on their body features.

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Think of a habitat as an animal's address! Just like you live in a house that protects you, animals live in places that suit their bodies perfectly.

hotspots.name: Terrestrial habitats
hotspots.description: Land-based homes like forests, deserts, and grasslands where animals like lions, deer, and elephants live

hotspots.name: Aquatic habitats
hotspots.description: Water-based homes like oceans, rivers, and ponds where fish, whales, and jellyfish live

hotspots.name: Amphibians
hotspots.description: Animals like frogs that can live both on land AND in water

hotspots.name: Arboreal habitats
hotspots.description: Tree-top homes where monkeys, squirrels, and many birds spend most of their time

hotspots.name: Aerial habitats
hotspots.description: The sky! Birds, bats, and flying insects spend much of their life in the air

animation.description: Visual infographic showing 5 habitat types with animals

scenes.title: Body Coverings - Feathers, Scales & Shells
scenes.narration: Animals have different types of body coverings that protect them from weather, enemies, and injuries. Let's explore the amazing variety of body armour in the animal kingdom!

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Imagine wearing a coat that keeps you warm AND protects you from enemies. That's exactly what body coverings do for animals!

hotspots.name: Feathers
hotspots.description: Birds have feathers that keep them warm and help them fly. Feathers are lightweight yet strong

hotspots.name: Scales
hotspots.description: Fish have slimy scales that help them glide through water. Reptiles have dry scales that prevent water loss

hotspots.name: Shell
hotspots.description: Snails, tortoises, and oysters carry hard shells on their backs for protection. They can hide inside when danger comes

hotspots.name: Wool
hotspots.description: Sheep have thick woolly coats that trap air and keep them warm in cold mountains

hotspots.name: Fur
hotspots.description: Polar bears and arctic foxes have thick fur that insulates them in freezing temperatures

animation.description: Animated display of feathers, scales, shells, wool, and fur

scenes.title: Body Coverings - Camouflage & Special Armour
scenes.narration: Some animals have body coverings that help them hide or defend themselves in clever ways. Nature has given them incredible survival tools!

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Have you ever tried to spot a zebra in tall grass? Their stripes make it really hard! This is called camouflage - blending in with the surroundings.

hotspots.name: Camouflage
hotspots.description: Zebras have stripes that confuse predators. Deer have brown coats that blend with forest floors

hotspots.name: Cuticle
hotspots.description: Insects like beetles and cockroaches have a hard outer layer called cuticle that acts like a suit of armour

hotspots.name: Quills
hotspots.description: Porcupines have sharp, pointed quills. When threatened, they raise their quills to scare away predators

hotspots.name: Armour plates
hotspots.description: Armadillos have bony plates covering their body. They can curl into a ball for complete protection

hotspots.name: Colour changes
hotspots.description: Chameleons can change their skin colour to match their surroundings and communicate

animation.description: Animated display of camouflage, cuticle, quills, armour plates

scenes.title: Body Coverings - Match the Animal
scenes.narration: Time for a matching challenge! Can you match each animal to its body covering?

dialogue.speaker: Buddi
dialogue.text: Let's see how well you remember! Match each animal with the covering that protects it. You've got this!

activity.title: Body Coverings - Match the Animal
activity.instructions: Time for a matching challenge! Can you match each animal to its body covering?

scenes.title: Eating Habits - Herbivores
scenes.narration: Animals eat different kinds of food, and their teeth and body parts are specially designed for their diet. Let's start with plant-eaters!

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Did you know you can tell what an animal eats just by looking at its teeth? Herbivores have flat, broad back teeth perfect for grinding leaves and grass!

hotspots.name: Herbivores are animals that eat only plants - like...
hotspots.description: Herbivores are animals that eat only plants - like cows, horses, deer, and rabbits

hotspots.name: They have sharp front teeth (incisors) to bite off...
hotspots.description: They have sharp front teeth (incisors) to bite off leaves and grass

hotspots.name: They have broad, flat back teeth (molars) to grind...
hotspots.description: They have broad, flat back teeth (molars) to grind and crush plant material

hotspots.name: Many herbivores have hard hooves to walk long dist...
hotspots.description: Many herbivores have hard hooves to walk long distances searching for food

hotspots.name: Herbivores often have long digestive systems becau...
hotspots.description: Herbivores often have long digestive systems because plants are harder to break down

animation.description: Animated comparison of herbivore teeth and feeding

scenes.title: Eating Habits - Carnivores, Omnivores & Special Feeders
scenes.narration: Now let's look at meat-eaters, animals that eat both plants and meat, and some very special feeders with unique body parts!

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: A lion's teeth are like sharp knives, while a butterfly drinks nectar through a straw-like tongue called a proboscis. Nature is so creative!

hotspots.name: Carnivores eat only meat - like lions, tigers, and...
hotspots.description: Carnivores eat only meat - like lions, tigers, and eagles. They have sharp, pointed teeth and strong claws or talons to catch prey

hotspots.name: Omnivores eat both plants and animals - like bears...
hotspots.description: Omnivores eat both plants and animals - like bears, crows, and humans. They have a mix of sharp and flat teeth

hotspots.name: Rodents like rats and squirrels have sharp front t...
hotspots.description: Rodents like rats and squirrels have sharp front teeth that never stop growing - perfect for gnawing hard nuts and seeds

hotspots.name: Butterflies have a long, coiled tube called probos...
hotspots.description: Butterflies have a long, coiled tube called proboscis to suck nectar from flowers

hotspots.name: Mosquitoes have a needle-like tube to pierce skin ...
hotspots.description: Mosquitoes have a needle-like tube to pierce skin and suck blood

animation.description: Animated display of carnivore teeth and special feeders

scenes.title: Eating Habits - Sort the Animals
scenes.narration: Can you sort these animals into the correct food group? Think about what each animal eats!

dialogue.speaker: Buddi
dialogue.text: Herbivore, Carnivore, or Omnivore? Let's sort them out! Remember - it's all about what they eat.

activity.title: Eating Habits - Sort the Animals
activity.instructions: Can you sort these animals into the correct food group? Think about what each animal eats!

scenes.title: Breathing - Lungs and Gills
scenes.narration: All animals need oxygen to survive, but they get it in very different ways! Let's explore how animals breathe.

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: You breathe through your nose and lungs, but did you know fish breathe through gills that filter oxygen from water? Amazing, right?

animation.description: Animated comparison of breathing methods - lungs, gills, spiracles

hotspots.name: Lungs
hotspots.description: Mammals, birds, and reptiles breathe using lungs. Air enters through nostrils, travels down the windpipe, and reaches the lungs where oxygen is absorbed by haemoglobin in blood

hotspots.name: Gills
hotspots.description: Fish and tadpoles breathe using gills. Water flows over thin gill filaments where oxygen is absorbed directly into the blood

hotspots.name: Blowholes
hotspots.description: Whales and dolphins are mammals that live in water but breathe air! They come to the surface and breathe through a blowhole on top of their head

hotspots.name: Frogs
hotspots.description: Tadpoles breathe through gills, but adult frogs use lungs on land and absorb oxygen through their moist skin in water

hotspots.name: Haemoglobin in our blood carries oxygen from lungs...
hotspots.description: Haemoglobin in our blood carries oxygen from lungs to every cell in the body

scenes.title: Breathing - Spiracles & Skin
scenes.narration: Some animals have truly unique breathing methods. Insects don't even have lungs! And earthworms breathe through their entire body surface.

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Insects have tiny holes called spiracles on the sides of their body. Air enters through these holes and travels through tubes called trachea directly to their cells!

hotspots.name: Spiracles
hotspots.description: Insects have small openings called spiracles on the sides of their body

hotspots.name: Tracheal system
hotspots.description: Air enters spiracles and travels through a network of tubes called trachea that deliver oxygen directly to body cells

hotspots.name: Skin breathing
hotspots.description: Earthworms breathe through their moist skin. Oxygen dissolves in the moisture and passes into their blood

hotspots.name: Earthworms must keep their skin moist at all times...
hotspots.description: Earthworms must keep their skin moist at all times - if they dry out, they cannot breathe and will die

hotspots.name: This is why you see earthworms come to the surface...
hotspots.description: This is why you see earthworms come to the surface after heavy rain - their underground burrows flood and they need air

animation.description: Animated diagram of insect spiracles and earthworm skin breathing

scenes.title: Breathing Methods - Match Up
scenes.narration: Let's test your knowledge! Match each animal to its breathing method.

dialogue.speaker: Buddi
dialogue.text: Lungs, gills, spiracles, skin, or blowhole? Match each animal to how it breathes!

activity.title: Breathing Methods - Match Up
activity.instructions: Let's test your knowledge! Match each animal to its breathing method.

scenes.title: Movement on Land
scenes.narration: Animals move in many different ways depending on their habitat and body structure. Let's explore land movement first!

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Some animals walk, some crawl, some hop, and some have six legs! The way an animal moves tells us a lot about where it lives.

hotspots.name: Four limbs
hotspots.description: Most land mammals like dogs, cats, horses, and elephants walk and run on four legs

hotspots.name: Crawling
hotspots.description: Reptiles like lizards crawl close to the ground. Snakes have no legs - they use scales, muscles, and a flexible backbone to slither

hotspots.name: Six legs
hotspots.description: All insects have six legs. Ants, beetles, and cockroaches scurry quickly on their six tiny legs

hotspots.name: Hopping
hotspots.description: Kangaroos have powerful back legs for hopping great distances. Grasshoppers and frogs also hop

hotspots.name: Snakes push against the ground with their belly sc...
hotspots.description: Snakes push against the ground with their belly scales and use S-shaped body movements to glide forward

animation.description: Animated showcase of walking, crawling, hopping, slithering

scenes.title: Movement - Flying and Swimming
scenes.narration: The sky and water present unique challenges for movement. Animals that fly or swim have special body features that make them experts in their environment!

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Birds have hollow bones to stay light, and fish have streamlined bodies to cut through water. Every feature has a purpose!

animation.description: Animated showcase of flying, swimming, and land movement adaptations

hotspots.name: Flying birds have
hotspots.description: streamlined body shape, hollow bones (lightweight), strong wings with feathers, and a tail for steering

hotspots.name: Swimming fish have
hotspots.description: fins for balance and direction, a streamlined body, scales with slime to reduce friction, and a tail fin for propulsion

hotspots.name: Webbed feet
hotspots.description: Ducks, frogs, and platypus have skin between their toes that works like paddles for swimming

hotspots.name: Flippers
hotspots.description: Penguins and turtles have flat, broad flippers instead of legs to push through water

hotspots.name: Bats are the only mammals that can truly fly - the...
hotspots.description: Bats are the only mammals that can truly fly - they have thin skin stretched between their long finger bones forming wings

scenes.title: Flightless Birds - A Special Group
scenes.narration: Not all birds can fly! Some birds have evolved to be expert runners or swimmers instead. These flightless birds are fascinating creatures.

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: The ostrich is the largest bird in the world but it cannot fly! Instead, it can run at speeds up to 70 km/h - faster than most cars in a city!

scenes.fun_fact: Penguins are flightless birds that are amazing swimmers. They use their wings as flippers and can dive over 500 meters deep! Other flightless birds include the emu (Australia), cassowary (New Guinea), and kiwi (New Zealand).

scenes.title: Movement - Sequence the Adaptation
scenes.narration: Let's arrange how a bird's body is adapted for flight, from most important to supporting features!

dialogue.speaker: Buddi
dialogue.text: Put these bird adaptations for flight in order from the most essential to supporting features!

activity.title: Movement - Sequence the Adaptation
activity.instructions: Let's arrange how a bird's body is adapted for flight, from most important to supporting features!

scenes.title: Animal Migration - Epic Journeys
scenes.narration: Some animals travel thousands of kilometres every year to find food, warmth, or safe places to have babies. This incredible journey is called migration!

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Imagine walking from India to Siberia! That's what some birds do - they fly thousands of kilometres twice a year. Let's learn about these amazing travellers.

hotspots.name: Siberian Crane
hotspots.description: Flies from Siberia (Russia) to India every winter to escape the extreme cold. They travel over 5000 km!

hotspots.name: Arctic Tern
hotspots.description: Has the longest migration - flies from the Arctic to Antarctica and back every year, covering about 70,000 km!

hotspots.name: Caribou
hotspots.description: Large deer that move south in winter to find food when their Arctic home gets too cold and snowy

hotspots.name: Salmon
hotspots.description: Born in rivers, swim to the ocean to grow, then return to the same river to lay eggs - an incredible journey!

hotspots.name: Monarch Butterflies
hotspots.description: Travel from Canada to Mexico (4000 km) every autumn. No single butterfly makes the full round trip - it takes 4 generations!

animation.description: Animated migration routes with directional arrows

scenes.title: More Migration Marvels
scenes.narration: India is home to some spectacular migration events too! Let's discover the amazing animals that visit our country.

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: Every year, thousands of Olive Ridley turtles come to the coast of Odisha to lay their eggs. This mass nesting event is called 'Arribada' - it's one of nature's greatest spectacles!

scenes.fun_fact: African antelopes (wildebeest) migrate in huge herds of over a million animals across the Serengeti to escape drought and find green grass. Olive Ridley sea turtles travel thousands of kilometres through the ocean to reach the beaches of Odisha, India, where they were born - to lay their own eggs!

scenes.title: Migration - Match the Journey
scenes.narration: Can you match each animal to its migration route or reason?

dialogue.speaker: Buddi
dialogue.text: These animals are world travellers! Match each one to where or why it migrates.

activity.title: Migration - Match the Journey
activity.instructions: Can you match each animal to its migration route or reason?

pairs.explanation: Olive Ridley Turtle → Ocean to Odisha coast

scenes.title: True or False Challenge
scenes.narration: Time to test what you've learned! Read each statement carefully and decide - is it TRUE or FALSE?

dialogue.speaker: Buddi
dialogue.text: I've prepared 8 tricky statements about animals. Think carefully before you answer - some of these might surprise you!

questions.explanation: Fish breathe using gills, not lungs. Gills extract oxygen dissolved in water.

questions.statement: Earthworms breathe through their moist skin.
questions.explanation: Correct! Earthworms absorb oxygen through their moist skin surface.
False! Ostrich, emu, penguin, and cassowary are flightless birds.

questions.statement: Insects breathe through small holes called spiracles.
questions.explanation: Correct! Spiracles are tiny openings on the sides of an insect's body that let air in.

questions.statement: Herbivores have sharp pointed teeth for tearing meat.
questions.explanation: False! Herbivores have broad, flat back teeth for grinding plants. Carnivores have sharp pointed teeth.

questions.statement: Whales breathe through a blowhole on top of their head.
questions.explanation: Correct! Whales are mammals that must surface to breathe air through their blowhole.

questions.statement: Snakes move using legs hidden under their scales.
questions.explanation: False! Snakes have no legs. They move using their scales, muscles, and flexible backbone.

questions.statement: The Arctic Tern has the longest migration route of any animal.
questions.explanation: Correct! Arctic Terns fly about 70,000 km every year from Arctic to Antarctic and back.

scenes.title: Final Quiz - Habitat Hero
scenes.narration: You've learned so much about animals and their amazing adaptations! Now it's time for the final quiz. Answer at least 7 out of 10 correctly to earn your Habitat Hero badge!

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: This is it - the final challenge! Show me everything you've learned about animals in their surroundings. Good luck, explorer!

questions.question: Which body covering helps a porcupine defend itself from predators?
What do insects use to breathe?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Spiracles and trachea

questions.question: Which type of teeth do herbivores use to grind plants?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Broad flat molars

questions.question: How do fish breathe underwater?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Through gills

questions.question: Which animal migrates from Canada to Mexico every year?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Monarch Butterfly

questions.question: What helps a snake move without legs?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Scales, muscles and flexible backbone

questions.question: How does an earthworm breathe?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Through moist skin

questions.question: What body feature helps ducks swim?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Webbed feet

questions.question: Where do Olive Ridley turtles come to lay eggs in India?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Odisha coast

scenes.title: Chapter Summary
scenes.narration: What an incredible journey through the animal kingdom! Let's review everything we discovered.

dialogue.speaker: Tara
dialogue.text: You've explored habitats, body coverings, eating habits, breathing methods, movement, and migration. You're a true animal expert now!

summary_points.title: Habitats
summary_points.text: Animals live in terrestrial, aquatic, arboreal, aerial, and amphibious habitats based on their body features.

summary_points.title: Body Coverings
summary_points.text: Animals have feathers, scales, shells, fur, wool, quills, cuticle, or armour plates for protection.

summary_points.title: Camouflage
summary_points.text: Zebras, deer, and chameleons use body patterns and colours to blend with surroundings and avoid predators.

summary_points.title: Eating Habits
summary_points.text: Herbivores have flat teeth for grinding, carnivores have sharp teeth for tearing, omnivores have both.

summary_points.title: Special Feeders
summary_points.text: Butterflies use proboscis for nectar, mosquitoes use needle-like tubes, rodents have ever-growing front teeth.

summary_points.title: Breathing
summary_points.text: Animals breathe through lungs (mammals), gills (fish), spiracles (insects), skin (earthworm), or blowhole (whales).

summary_points.title: Movement
summary_points.text: Animals walk, crawl, slither, hop, fly, or swim using specialized body parts adapted to their habitat.

summary_points.title: Migration
summary_points.text: Animals like Siberian Crane, Arctic Tern, Monarch Butterfly, and Olive Ridley Turtle travel thousands of km for survival.

scenes.title: Badge Awarded - Habitat Hero!
scenes.narration: Congratulations, explorer! You've completed the Animals in Their Surroundings chapter and earned the Habitat Hero badge!

dialogue.speaker: Buddi
dialogue.text: You did it! You're now a certified Habitat Hero! You know all about how animals live, eat, breathe, and move. Wear this badge with pride!

badge.name: Habitat Hero
badge.description: Awarded for mastering animal habitats, body coverings, eating habits, breathing methods, movement, and migration!
